hero.title: Ziad Rafik Bouraoui
hero.subtitle: Computer Science Student — IoT & AI
hero.focus: Building secure industrial IoT systems and edge AI applications. Currently in my 4th year at ESTIN, specializing in AI & Data Science.

about.title: About
about.description: I'm a Computer Science student specializing in AI and Data Science, with a hands-on focus on the Internet of Things, connected industrial systems, and intelligent data analysis.
about.detail: I enjoy designing solutions that combine sensors, automation, and data processing — from TinyML models running on $5 microcontrollers to full-stack industrial monitoring platforms. My projects live at the intersection of hardware, protocols, and intelligence.

projects.title: Projects
projects.intro: A selection of projects spanning edge AI, industrial IoT, protocol gateways, and robotics simulation.

featured.title: Edge Predictive Maintenance
featured.tagline: TinyML anomaly detection that fits in 25KB and runs on a $5 ESP32 — no cloud, no server.
featured.problem: Industrial rotating equipment fails gradually, and the early warning signs show up as subtle changes in vibration signatures. Traditional predictive maintenance either streams raw sensor data to the cloud (expensive, latency-bound, connectivity-dependent) or requires dedicated industrial vibration analyzers that can't be deployed at scale on every machine.
featured.approach: Move the entire detection pipeline onto the sensor node itself. The ESP32 acquires vibration data, extracts frequency features via FFT, runs a neural-network autoencoder for anomaly detection, and raises a local alert — all within ~215ms and ~55KB of RAM. 100% edge computing, no cloud required.

components.role: Model training, threshold calibration, C header generation

decisions: Autoencoder over a classifier — unsupervised on normal data only, no need for labeled fault data for every failure mode.
FFT as the feature, not raw waveform — frequency-domain features are more compact and discriminative for vibration faults, shrinking model input from 128 to 64 dimensions.
8-dim latent bottleneck keeps the network small enough to deploy as a 24.9KB TFLite model.
Bake everything into firmware constants — the trained model, scaler, and threshold are code-generated into C headers, so the ESP32 needs zero runtime configuration.
TF-free demo path — weights extracted to JSON and forward pass reimplemented in pure NumPy, so the Streamlit dashboard runs without TensorFlow installed.

deepDive.title: Anomaly detection via reconstruction error
deepDive.body: The autoencoder is trained on only healthy vibration spectra. At runtime, the ESP32 reconstructs each incoming spectrum; if the reconstruction error (MSE) exceeds a threshold, the signal is flagged anomalous. The threshold is auto-derived as the 95th percentile of normal-data reconstruction error (1.151) — no manual tuning per deployment.
deepDive.code:
TFLite → Autoencoder (64→32→16→8→16→32→64)
Detection → MSE > 1.151 ? ANOMALY : NORMAL

deepDive.title: Bridging PC-trained Keras to a microcontroller
deepDive.body: A custom code generator converts the TFLite binary into an alignas(8) hex array in a C header, along with a separate scaler_params.h containing the threshold and scaler mean/scale arrays. This eliminates any runtime parsing or SD-card file dependency on the device.

deepDive.title: On-device inference loop
deepDive.body: Each cycle: acquire 128 samples (128ms), compute FFT with Hamming window (15ms), normalize (2ms), run TFLite inference (~85ms), compare MSE to threshold, light LED on anomaly. Total cycle latency ~215ms — well under the 500ms target.
deepDive.code:
Inférence #10 | Error: 0.023456 | Anomalies: 1/10 | Temps: 87ms (Moy: 89.3ms)
ANOMALIE DÉTECTÉE!

demoFeatures: ESP32 serial monitor streams live inference stats with anomaly count and timing
On-board LED lights up immediately on anomaly detection
Works with simulated signals out-of-the-box or real ADXL345 accelerometer via one flag
Streamlit dashboard with real-time waveform, FFT spectrum, error gauge, and rolling history
Full validation suite asserting model < 100KB, accuracy > 80%, and C header integrity
lessons: Fitting a neural net in ~160KB of free DRAM required a fixed 30KB tensor arena and carefully sized FFT buffers — memory tuning is documented for retuning.
Bridging a PC-trained Keras model to a microcontroller required a custom code generator emitting C headers — no runtime file I/O on the device.
Decoupling the demo from TensorFlow by reimplementing the forward pass in ~45 lines of NumPy made the cloud dashboard cheap to host and fast to cold-start.
Threshold engineering without labels for every fault mode — the 95th-percentile approach auto-calibrates and the README documents the sensitivity tradeoff for operators.

featured.title: Secure IoT Mini-Plant
featured.tagline: A fully containerized simulation of a secure industrial IoT plant — 6 devices stream telemetry over mutual-TLS MQTT into a real-time Grafana monitoring stack.
featured.problem: Industrial IoT deployments live or die on three things: secure device-to-broker communication, reliable time-series ingestion at scale, and real-time observability with alerting. Most tutorials hand-wave the security layer or bolt on monitoring as an afterthought.
featured.approach: Build an end-to-end, production-shaped IIoT reference stack where every device-to-broker message is encrypted and mutually authenticated (mTLS), heterogeneous sensor data is normalized into a single time-series store, and operators get a live auto-provisioned Grafana dashboard with threshold-based alerting.

components.role: MQTTS on 8883 with mTLS, internal plaintext on 1883
Subscribes to iot/plant/#, writes InfluxDB line protocol

decisions: Custom Python bridge over Node-RED — a ~90-line subscriber/writer has fewer moving parts, smaller container, and full programmatic control over field filtering and line-protocol construction.
Two-listener broker design — mTLS on 8883 for devices, internal plaintext on 1883 for backend services inside the Docker network, avoiding redundant TLS on the trusted internal fabric.
Security first, security enforced — TLS 1.2 is mandatory with mutual certificate verification; a 5-test automated security suite proves plaintext and bad-cert connections are refused.
Infrastructure-as-code provisioning — Grafana datasource and dashboard are provisioned from YAML/JSON on startup, no manual UI configuration.
Container hardening — simulator runs as a non-root user (uid 1000), private keys are chmod 600, certs/ is gitignored.

deepDive.title: Mutual TLS for every device
deepDive.body: The external broker port (8883) requires TLS 1.2 with mutual certificate verification (require_certificate true). An automated 5-test security suite validates that plaintext connections are refused, wrong-cert connections are refused, valid mTLS succeeds, TLS 1.2 is enforced, and strict certificate verification is active.
deepDive.code:
listener 8883
certfile /certs/server.crt
keyfile /certs/server.key
cafile /certs/ca.crt
require_certificate true
tls_version tlsv1.2

deepDive.title: Python bridge: MQTT to InfluxDB
deepDive.body: A ~90-line Python service subscribes to iot/plant/#, parses JSON payloads, and writes InfluxDB line-protocol points via HTTP. Smaller attack surface than Node-RED, fewer containers, fully scriptable.
deepDive.code:
def on_message(client, userdata, msg):
    payload = json.loads(msg.payload.decode())
    device_type = msg.topic.split('/')[2]
    fields = {k: v for k, v in payload.items() if isinstance(v, (int, float))}
    line = f"{device_type},device_id={payload['device_id']} " + \
           ",".join(f"{k}={v}" for k, v in fields.items())
    requests.post(f"{INFLUX_URL}/api/v2/write?bucket=iot-data&precision=s",
                  data=line, headers={"Authorization": f"Token {INFLUX_TOKEN}"})

deepDive.title: 6 simulated devices with anomaly injection
deepDive.body: Each simulator is a containerized Python process emitting realistic telemetry: 2 production machines (5% random failure), temperature sensor (2% spike chance, alert at 80°C), pressure sensor (3% overpressure, alert at 5 bar), vibration sensor (4% anomaly on random axis), and an energy meter. MQTT topic scheme: iot/plant/{device_type}/{device_id}.

demoFeatures: Single-command bring-up: docker-compose up -d spins the entire 8-service stack
Live Grafana dashboard at localhost:3001 with 8 auto-provisioned panels
Real-time anomaly injection visible as dashboard blips within seconds
Automated TLS security test suite with 100% pass rate
Horizontally scalable — adding a device = duplicating one compose service block
lessons: Node-RED was replaced with a ~90-line Python bridge — off-the-shelf glue tools add operational weight; a thin purpose-built service is clearer for a fixed pipeline.
Certificate SAN must cover both the Docker DNS alias and localhost for validation from inside and outside the network.
depends_on only waits for container start, not service readiness — always code MQTT clients to retry with backoff.
Bake least-privilege into the Docker image (non-root user, chmod 600 keys), not the runtime.

featured.title: PLC Gateway
featured.tagline: An industrial protocol gateway bridging legacy Modbus TCP PLCs to modern OPC UA, with real-time analytics and Grafana visualization.
featured.problem: Modern factories run a mix of legacy and modern equipment. Older PLCs expose data only via Modbus TCP — a simple, register-based protocol — while modern SCADA/MES/IIoT systems expect OPC UA, a richer, standardized, semantically-tagged protocol. Bridging the two by hand is tedious: poll registers, decode fixed-point scalings, reassemble 32-bit counters split across two 16-bit registers, map bitfields to meaningful states.
featured.approach: Build a self-contained, Dockerized pipeline that reads live data from a (simulated) industrial PLC over Modbus, exposes it as OPC UA, then streams it through Node-RED for processing into InfluxDB and Grafana dashboards — all in one reproducible docker compose up.

components.role: Simulates PLC with 11 holding registers, sine-wave + noise
Modbus→OPC UA bridge, fixed-point decode, auto-reconnect
Derived analytics: power, rates, moving averages, anomaly flags

decisions: Cyclic polling with auto-reconnect — polls Modbus every 1s, closes socket and retries after 3s on failure, robust against PLC restarts.
Lazy OPC UA variable getters — each node uses a get() closure reading in-memory plcData, so the address space always reflects the latest poll.
Fixed-point decoding at the gateway — temperature in 0.1°C, pressure in 0.01 Bar, vibration in 0.01 mm/s, all decoded at the bridge layer.
32-bit counter reassembly — production count split across two 16-bit registers, recombined with (high << 16) | low.
Separation of translation vs. analytics — gateway stays a pure protocol bridge (<100ms), all derived computation lives in Node-RED.

deepDive.title: Protocol impedance matching: Modbus → OPC UA
deepDive.body: Modbus is flat 16-bit registers with implicit, out-of-band scaling. OPC UA is a typed, hierarchical, browsable object model. The gateway invents the address-space structure (PLC_Data folder + typed variables) and encodes all fixed-point scaling knowledge in one place.
deepDive.code:
// OPC UA address space exposed under Objects/PLC_Data/
MotorSpeed    (Double)
Temperature   (Double, ×0.1 °C)
Pressure      (Double, ×0.01 Bar)
Vibration     (Double, ×0.01 mm/s)
Current       (Double, ×0.1 A)
ProductionCount (UInt32, (reg[6]<<16)|reg[7])
MachineState  (String: Stop/Start/Run/Alarm)
MotorOn       (Boolean, status & 0x01)
AlarmActive   (Boolean, status & 0x08)

deepDive.title: Bitfield-to-semantics mapping
deepDive.body: A single status register holds multiple boolean states packed into bits. The gateway unpacks them with masks into discrete OPC UA Boolean nodes so downstream consumers don't need to know bit positions.

deepDive.title: Derived analytics in Node-RED
deepDive.body: Rather than dump raw values, Node-RED computes three-phase power (√3 × 400 × I × 0.85 / 1000), production rate per hour, moving averages, standard deviation, and deviation-from-mean anomaly flags — all before writing to InfluxDB.

demoFeatures: No physical PLC needed — simulator generates sine-wave + noise with 2% random alarm trigger
Single command: docker compose build && docker compose up -d
9-panel Grafana dashboard with gauges, stat panels, and time-series (5s auto-refresh)
221-line automated test harness covering ports, containers, Modbus reads, conversion logs, InfluxDB writes, and Grafana dashboards
Temperature alarms, vibration spikes, and state transitions appear naturally within minutes
lessons: Protocol impedance mismatch between Modbus (flat 16-bit, implicit scaling) and OPC UA (typed, hierarchical, browsable) required inventing the address-space structure and encoding scaling in one place.
Reassembling 32-bit values across two 16-bit registers needs correct endianness — (high << 16) | low — a classic Modbus pitfall.
Connection resilience on industrial networks — close-on-error + delayed auto-reconnect beats crashing, with winston structured logging for observability.
Reproducibility without hardware — building a faithful PLC simulator (sine-wave + noise + stochastic alarms) made the entire stack demo-able on a laptop.

other.title: ESP Cloud
other.description: Full-stack IoT platform: C++ embedded web server on ESP32 with REST + WebSocket API, driven by a Next.js dashboard. Hot-registerable hardware components that survive reboots.

other.title: Industrial Monitoring Stack
other.description: Self-contained monitoring pipeline: MQTT → Node-RED → InfluxDB → Grafana, fully containerized with zero-config auto-provisioning of all services.

other.title: Smart Parking IoT
other.description: ESP32 + 6× HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensors serving live parking-bay occupancy as JSON, with an animated Next.js dashboard and transducer auto-detection at boot.

other.title: Skydio X2 Drone Simulator
other.description: Physics-based 6-DOF drone flight simulator in MuJoCo with a modular PID controller (anti-windup, derivative-on-measurement, low-pass filter), real-time keyboard flight, and an obstacle course.

details: Participated in a project to improve information systems for energy data management and analysis.
Contributed to internal process automation and reliable data exchange between applications.
Discovered the operations of a major energy company and the challenges of digitalization and operational efficiency.
